title: Nigeria Arrests 15 Over Vicious Festival Assault Videos as Outrage Mounts
description: Fifteen people, including an event organizer, have been arrested in Nigeria after viral videos showed young men sexually assaulting women at a local festival, prompting nationwide outrage and the hashtag #StopRapingWomen.
lede: The alarming incidents occurred during the Alue-Do fertility festival in the city of Ozoro, located in Nigeria's southern Delta state. Viral footage captured disturbing scenes of mobs of young men openly attacking women who were walking alone in public spaces. In some instances, the assaults were being recorded by bystanders rather than stopped.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: The alarming incidents occurred during the Alue-Do fertility festival in the city of Ozoro, located in Nigeria's southern Delta state. Viral footage captured disturbing scenes of mobs of young men openly attacking women who were walking alone in public spaces. In some instances, the assaults were being recorded by bystanders rather than stopped.

Delta state police acted swiftly following the public outcry, detaining 15 suspects identified from the videos. The arrested individuals, which include a community leader widely named as the event's organizer, have been transferred to the state Criminal Investigation Department (CID) for further questioning.

sections.heading: The Police Response
paragraphs: Local police spokesperson Bright Edafe described the scenes depicted in the videos as "alarming, disgusting and embarrassing." However, authorities have pushed back against claims circulating online that Alue-Do was a "rape festival."

According to Edafe, no formal reports of rape have been filed in connection with the events in Ozoro. "We have spoken to four girls and all of them said nobody raped them," he told Channels TV on Monday.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: Despite the lack of formal rape complaints, rights groups emphasize that the acts documented on video—including forced stripping, grabbing, and public humiliation—constitute serious gender-based violence under Nigerian law.

The incidents have highlighted underlying cultural tensions. Witnesses and local activists reported that women were explicitly warned not to go outside during certain parts of the festival, raising concerns that the targeting of women was either organized or tacitly tolerated under the guise of tradition.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Traditional and political leaders have scrambled to distance the festival from the violence. The King of Ozoro firmly rejected suggestions that the Alue-Do festival condones sexual assault, stating the fertility rite was "misinterpreted and abused by some youths." Traditional leaders acknowledged that women were harassed but insisted the acts were criminal deviations, not cultural norms.

The Delta state government echoed this sentiment, officially stating that no recognized festival in the region permits sexual assault and declaring that all perpetrators should be treated strictly as criminals.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: The national spotlight is now on the Nigerian justice system to ensure accountability. Nigeria's First Lady, Oluremi Tinubu, who has personal roots in Delta state, issued a strong condemnation of the assaults.

In a signed statement, Tinubu stressed that no culture justifies violating women and girls. She praised the police for the rapid arrests, urged security agencies to prosecute all offenders, and encouraged the victims to step forward to seek necessary medical and psychological support. The country waits to see what charges will officially be brought against the 15 suspects currently in CID custody.
More coverage coming. This is a developing story.
